content: Familial adenomatous polyposis is inherited in an {{c1::autosomal dominant}} pattern.
Familial adenomatous polyposis features innumerable {{c1::premalignant adenomatous polyps}} in the colon.
In familial adenomatous polyposis, adenomatous polyps also occur to a lesser extent in the {{c1::small bowel}}.
The lifetime colon cancer risk in familial adenomatous polyposis approaches {{c1::100%}}.
The average age for colon cancer development in familial adenomatous polyposis is about {{c1::39 years}}.
Standard colon cancer prevention in familial adenomatous polyposis is {{c1::prophylactic colectomy}}.
Gastric polyps in familial adenomatous polyposis are usually {{c1::hyperplastic}} and are {{c2::not premalignant}}.
Gardner syndrome is a variant of {{c1::familial adenomatous polyposis}}.
Gardner syndrome adds {{c1::desmoid tumors, osteomas, papillary thyroid cancer, and epidermoid cysts}} to colon polyps in FAP.
The mnemonic for Gardner syndrome extracolonic findings is {{c1::DOPE Gardner}}.
In the Gardner syndrome mnemonic, DOPE stands for {{c1::desmoid tumors, osteomas, papillary thyroid cancer, and epidermoid cysts}}.
Turcot syndrome is a variant of {{c1::familial adenomatous polyposis}}.
Turcot syndrome adds {{c1::CNS tumors}} to colon polyps in FAP.
The CNS tumors associated with Turcot syndrome include {{c1::gliomas and medulloblastomas}}.
The mnemonic for Turcot syndrome is {{c1::TURbans go over your head}}.

Most likely diagnosis on this coronal CT?

{{c1::familial adenomatous polyposis}}
Lynch syndrome is also called {{c1::hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome}}.
Hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome is caused by {{c1::DNA mismatch repair defects}}.
Hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome causes colon cancer through {{c1::microsatellite instability}}.
The colon cancer risk in hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome is {{c1::less than in FAP}}.
Colon polyps in hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome are {{c1::adenomatous}}, similar to FAP.
Hereditary nonpolyposis colon cancer syndrome is associated with {{c1::endometrial, gastric, small bowel, liver, and biliary malignancies}}.
